section.title: The Vine and the Branches
section.sectionRationale: The Upper Room Discourse continues with the last I am saying: I am the true vine. Jesus pictures the union of Himself and His people as a vine and its branches, from which alone fruit can come, and calls His disciples to remain in Him and in His love. He raises them from servants to friends, roots their fruitfulness in His choosing, and warns that a world which hated Him will hate them too, even as the Spirit and they together will testify to Him.
section.thesis: Jesus is the true vine and His people are the branches, so that fruitful life comes only by remaining in Him and in His love, keeping His command to love one another; He calls them friends and chose them to bear lasting fruit, warning that the world which hated Him will hate them, while the Spirit and they together bear witness to Him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the true vine and the branches and the call to remain and bear fruit; the greater love and the raising of servants to friends chosen to bear lasting fruit; and the hatred of the world and the twin witness of the Spirit and the disciples.

units.label: I am the true vine
units.note: remain in Me and bear much fruit

verses.text: “{{egoeimi|I am}} the true {{ampelos|vine}}, and My Father is the keeper of the vineyard.
He cuts off every branch in Me that bears no {{karpos|fruit}}, and every branch that does bear fruit, He prunes to make it even more fruitful.
You are already clean because of the word I have spoken to you.
{{meno|Remain}} in Me, and I will remain in you. Just as no branch can bear fruit by itself unless it remains in the vine, neither can you bear fruit unless you remain in Me.
I am the vine and you are the branches. The one who remains in Me, and I in him, will bear much fruit. For apart from Me you can do nothing.
If anyone does not remain in Me, he is like a branch that is thrown away and withers. Such branches are gathered up, thrown into the fire, and burned.
If you remain in Me and My words remain in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you.
This is to My Father’s glory, that you bear much fruit, proving yourselves to be My disciples.
As the Father has loved Me, so have I loved you. Remain in My love.
If you keep My commandments, you will remain in My love, just as I have kept My Father’s commandments and remain in His love.
I have told you these things so that My joy may be in you and your joy may be complete.
This is My commandment, that you love one another as I have loved you.
Greater love has no one than this, that he lay down his life for his friends.
You are My friends if you do what I command you.
No longer do I call you servants, for a servant does not understand what his master is doing. But I have called you friends, because everything I have learned from My Father I have made known to you.
You did not choose Me, but I chose you. And I appointed you to go and bear fruit, fruit that will remain, so that whatever you ask the Father in My name, He will give you.
This is My command to you: Love one another.

units.label: The hatred of the world
units.note: the Spirit and you will testify

verses.text: If the world hates you, understand that it hated Me first.
If you were of the world, it would love you as its own. Instead, the world hates you, because you are not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world.
Remember the word that I spoke to you: ‘No servant is greater than his master.’ If they persecuted Me, they will persecute you as well; if they kept My word, they will keep yours as well.
But they will treat you like this because of My name, since they do not know the One who sent Me.
If I had not come and spoken to them, they would not be guilty of sin. Now, however, they have no excuse for their sin.
Whoever hates Me hates My Father as well.
If I had not done among them the works that no one else did, they would not be guilty of sin; but now they have seen and hated both Me and My Father.
But this is to fulfill what is written in their Law: ‘They hated Me without reason.’
When the Advocate comes, whom I will send to you from the Father, the Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father, He will testify about Me.
And you also must testify, because you have been with Me from the beginning.

egoeimi.gloss: I am. The seventh and last of the great I am sayings, I am the true vine, completes the series. As with the others, the phrase names both a rich image and the divine self; Jesus is the true vine over against unfruitful Israel, the source from which all fruitful life in His people must flow.

ampelos.gloss: Vine. Israel was often pictured as God's vine or vineyard, frequently a fruitless and disappointing one. Jesus declares Himself the true vine, the faithful Israel who bears the fruit God sought, and the living stock into which His people are grafted so that they too may bear fruit that pleases the Father.

karpos.gloss: Fruit. The fruit the Father seeks is the life that flows from union with Christ: love, obedience, answered prayer, and the ingathering of others, fruit that lasts. It is not self-produced but the natural yield of a branch that remains in the vine, and its abundance brings glory to the Father and proves genuine discipleship.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, stay. The key word of the chapter, used repeatedly: remain in Me, and I in you. Abiding is a settled, continuing union with Christ, kept by holding to His word and His love; apart from it a branch can do nothing but wither. To remain in Christ is the secret of all fruitfulness.

basic.context: Still in the upper room, Jesus gives His last I am saying: I am the true vine, and My Father is the gardener. His disciples are branches, and the one lesson pressed again and again is, remain in Me. A branch cut off from the vine can do nothing and withers; a branch that remains bears much fruit. Jesus calls them to remain in His love by keeping His commands, above all the command to love one another. He lifts them from servants to friends, reminds them He chose them to bear lasting fruit, and warns that the world that hated Him will hate them too, though the Spirit and they will testify to Him.
basic.hermeneutics: The vine imagery draws on the Old Testament picture of Israel as God's vine, often fruitless; Jesus is the true vine who succeeds where Israel failed. The key word is remain, or abide. The key questions are, where does fruitful life come from, and how do we stay connected to Christ. The answer is union with Him, kept by His word and His love. The warning about fruitless branches calls for self-examination, and the promise of friendship and lasting fruit gives deep assurance.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a few words. Jesus is the true ampelos, the vine, the last ego eimi saying. His people are branches meant to bear karpos, fruit. And the repeated command is meno, remain, abide in Me and in My love, for apart from Him they can do nothing.
basic.typology: The vine fulfills the image of Israel as God's vineyard, now realized in the true vine, Christ, and His people grafted into Him. The friendship with God echoes Abraham, called the friend of God, now extended to all who obey Christ.
basic.christ: Christ is the true vine, the source of all fruitful life, whose love the branches must remain in. He lays down His life for His friends, the greatest love, and He chose His people to bear lasting fruit. The world's hatred of Him is passed on to His own.
basic.god: God the Father is the gardener who prunes for fruit and is glorified when His people bear much fruit. He loves the Son, and the Son's love for His people mirrors it. To hate the Son is to hate the Father.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 5 and Psalm 80 picture Israel as God's vine. John 13 gave the command to love. Galatians 5 names the fruit of the Spirit. James 2 calls Abraham the friend of God.
basic.oneStory: Where Israel the vine failed to bear fruit, Jesus the true vine bears the fruit God sought and makes His people fruitful branches in Himself. Remaining in Him, loved and chosen as friends, they bear lasting fruit for the Father's glory, even as the hostile world rages, and the Spirit joins their witness to Christ.

context.literary: The continuation of the Upper Room Discourse, built on the extended metaphor of the vine and branches, then flowing into teaching on love, friendship, election, and the world's hatred. The command to love one another brackets the middle section, and the chapter ends by introducing the twin witness of the Spirit and the disciples, which chapter 16 develops. Abide is the unifying refrain.
context.historical: The vine was a national symbol of Israel, even adorning the temple, and the prophets repeatedly used the vineyard image to indict Israel's fruitlessness. Viticulture involved cutting away dead wood and pruning fruitful branches to increase yield, illuminating the Father's work. Calling disciples friends rather than servants was a striking elevation in a hierarchical culture, and the warning of persecution prepared them for the hostility they would soon face.
context.canonical: I am the true vine fulfills and transforms Isaiah 5:1-7 and Psalm 80:8-16, where Israel is God's vine. The fruit of abiding aligns with the fruit of the Spirit in Galatians 5:22-23. Friendship with God recalls Abraham, James 2:23, and anticipates the intimacy of the new covenant. The Spirit's witness together with the apostles' testimony frames the mission of the church, Acts 5:32, and the theme of the world's hatred runs on into 1 John.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal Jesus as the true vine and to call His disciples to remain in Him and in His love as the only source of fruitful life, to assure them of His friendship and their election to bear lasting fruit, and to prepare them for the world's hatred with the promise of the Spirit's witness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vine and branches teach an abiding truth for all believers: fruitfulness comes only through union with Christ, sustained by His word and love. The command to love one another and to abide is prescriptive for every disciple, while the specific warnings of persecution were first addressed to the eleven yet extend to the church.

debates.question: Who are the branches that are cut off and burned in verses 2 and 6?

views.label: Merely professing, not truly united
views.case: Those cut off are branches in name only, whose lack of fruit shows they never truly abided; the passage warns against a false, fruitless attachment to Christ that is not real union.

views.label: A solemn warning to genuine disciples to persevere
views.case: The language addresses the disciples directly and calls them to remain, so it functions as a real warning that spurs true believers to keep abiding, consistent with the assurance of election in verse 16.

debates.bearing: Both preserve the passage's force: it warns that fruitless attachment ends in judgment and calls all who claim Christ to prove their union by remaining and bearing fruit, while verse 16 grounds true disciples in Christ's own choosing.

debates.question: What is the fruit Jesus speaks of?

views.label: Christlike character and love
views.case: The immediate context stresses love, obedience, and joy, so the fruit is above all the character of Christ formed in the branch, the fruit of the Spirit growing from union with Him.

views.label: Character together with mission and converts
views.case: Verse 16 speaks of being sent to bear lasting fruit and links it to prayer and witness, so the fruit also includes the ingathering of others through the disciples' testimony.

debates.bearing: The fruit is both: the character of Christ and the fruit of mission flow from the same abiding, so a life truly joined to the vine yields both godliness and gospel harvest to the Father's glory.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is built on the last of the seven great sayings, ego eimi, I am the true ampelos, the vine. Against the Old Testament picture of Israel as a disappointing vine, Jesus is the true vine, the faithful stock into which His people are grafted. Their purpose is to bear karpos, fruit, the love, obedience, joy, and harvest that please the Father, and this fruit is not self-produced but the natural yield of a branch that stays connected. Hence the repeated command, meno, remain, abide in Me and in My love, for apart from Him they can do nothing, but the one who remains bears much fruit to the Father's glory.

typology.type: Israel as God's vine, Isaiah 5 and Psalm 80
typology.fulfillment: Christ the true vine, who bears the fruit Israel failed to bear and makes His people fruitful in Himself, John 15:1.
The disciples raised from servants to friends of Jesus, taken into the Father's counsel, John 15:15 and James 2:23.
The Father who prunes fruitful branches and is glorified by much fruit, John 15:1-2 and 8.

study.christ: Christ is the true vine, the source of all fruitful life for His people, who can do nothing apart from Him. He is the one who loves His own as the Father loves Him and calls them to remain in that love. He is the friend who lays down His life for His friends, the greatest love there is, and who has raised His disciples from servants to friends by sharing with them all He has learned from the Father. He is the one who chose them, not they Him, and appointed them to bear lasting fruit. And He is the one the world hated first, whose rejection His followers will share.
study.god: God the Father is the keeper of the vineyard, who tends the branches, cutting away the fruitless and pruning the fruitful so they bear more, and who is glorified when His people bear much fruit. He loves the Son, and that love overflows in the Son's love for His people. The Father sends the Spirit of truth, who proceeds from Him, to testify to the Son. To see and reject the Son is to hate the Father also, for the two are one, so that the world's hostility to Jesus is ultimately hostility to God Himself.

crossReferences.note: The song of the vineyard, where Israel is God's vine that yielded only wild grapes, the backdrop to the true vine.
You brought a vine out of Egypt, a prayer for God's vine, fulfilled in Christ the true vine.
The fruit of the Spirit, love, joy, peace, the fruit borne by branches abiding in Christ.
Abraham was called a friend of God, the friendship now extended to those who obey Christ.

study.oneStory: Where Israel, planted as God's vine, failed to bear the fruit He sought, Jesus stands as the true vine who bears that fruit and makes His people fruitful branches grafted into Himself. Remaining in Him, loved by Him and chosen as His friends, they bear lasting fruit that glorifies the Father, the very harvest the vineyard was always meant to yield. The hostile world rages against the vine and its branches as it always has, but the Spirit of truth joins the disciples' witness, and the mission of bearing fruit and testifying to Christ goes on until the harvest is complete.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by I am the true vine and you are the branches?

options.text: Fruitful life flows only from union with Him; apart from Him the branches can do nothing.
options.note: Verses 4 and 5 tie all fruit to remaining in Christ the vine.

options.text: Believers can bear fruit on their own if they try hard enough.
options.note: Jesus says apart from Him you can do nothing; fruit comes only from union with Him.

options.text: It mainly teaches that we should stay busy for God.
options.note: It does call for fruitfulness, but the point is dependent union with Christ, not mere busyness.

options.text: Believers depend on Christ to be fruitful.
options.note: True, but it understates the total dependence, that apart from Him they can do nothing at all.

quiz.q: What is the repeated command at the heart of the vine imagery?

options.text: Remain, or abide, in Christ and in His love.
options.note: The word remain runs through verses 4 to 10 as the key to fruitfulness.

options.text: Work harder to produce fruit by effort.
options.note: Fruitfulness involves real obedience, but the command is to remain in Christ, from whom the fruit flows, not self-powered effort.

options.text: Separate from Christ to prove one's independence.
options.note: The opposite; separation from the vine means withering.

options.text: Stay closely connected to Jesus.
options.note: True, but it does not capture the rich sense of abiding in His love by keeping His word.

quiz.q: How does Jesus describe the greatest love?

options.text: To lay down one's life for one's friends, as He was about to do.
options.note: Verse 13 defines the greatest love, which Jesus embodies at the cross.

options.text: To feel strong affection for many people.
options.note: Affection has a place, but the greatest love Jesus names is the sacrificial act of laying down one's life.

options.text: To keep religious rules carefully.
options.note: The measure of love is self-sacrifice, not rule-keeping.

options.text: To love others sacrificially at real cost.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific and highest form, laying down one's life for one's friends.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say about how the disciples came to be His?

options.text: You did not choose Me, but I chose you and appointed you to bear lasting fruit.
options.note: Verse 16 grounds their standing in Christ's choosing, not their own.

options.text: They responded in faith when Jesus called them.
options.note: Their faith was real, but Jesus stresses the prior truth, you did not choose Me, I chose you.

options.text: They chose Jesus after weighing the options.
options.note: The verse explicitly reverses this: I chose you.

options.text: Jesus chose them to go and bear fruit.
options.note: True, but it omits the pointed contrast, you did not choose Me, and the lasting nature of the fruit.

quiz.q: What does Jesus tell the disciples to expect from the world?

options.text: The world will hate them, because it hated Him first.
options.note: Verses 18 and 19 warn that the world's hatred of Christ falls on His followers.

options.text: The world will welcome and honor them.
options.note: He warns of hatred, not welcome, from the world.

options.text: The world will merely tolerate them at a distance.
options.note: It is not indifference; Jesus warns of active hatred, as the world hated Him first.

options.text: They may face persecution for His name.
options.note: True, but it softens the direct statement that the world will hate them as it hated Him.

questions: Jesus says apart from Him you can do nothing. Where are you trying to bear fruit by your own effort rather than by remaining in Him?
The Father prunes fruitful branches to make them more fruitful. How might a painful season in your life be His loving pruning rather than rejection?
Jesus calls His disciples friends, not servants. How does it change your walk with Him to know you are counted His friend?
You did not choose Me, but I chose you. How does resting in Christ's choosing of you steady your assurance and free you to serve?
Jesus warned that the world would hate His followers as it hated Him. Where are you tempted to soften your allegiance to Christ to avoid the world's disapproval?

translationNotes.note: I am the true vine draws on Isaiah 5 and Psalm 80, where Israel is God's vine; Jesus presents Himself as the true vine that bears the fruit Israel did not.
No servant is greater than his master recalls John 13:16; because Jesus was persecuted, His servants will be too.
They hated Me without reason fulfills the psalms of the righteous sufferer; the BSB footnote points to Psalms 35:19, 38:19, and 69:4.
Advocate renders parakletos; the BSB footnote gives Comforter, Helper, or Counselor. The Spirit of truth proceeds from the Father and testifies about the Son.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 15:1-27. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. Em dashes in verses 16 (fruit that will remain) and 26 (the Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father) were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
